title: FATF Mid-Year Review of Emerging Money Laundering Typologies, 2025
content: Section 4.7 — Cross-Chain Laundering. During the reporting period, FATF members observed an increase in the use of cross-chain bridges and atomic swap services to obfuscate the path of illicit proceeds. Subjects deposit funds at a centralized exchange operating one virtual asset blockchain (e.g., a Bitcoin-native exchange), then route the assets through decentralized bridge protocols to a second chain (e.g., Tron, Solana, or an EVM L2), commonly using privacy-enhancing techniques such as mixers or batching contracts. The chain-hopping pattern complicates the chain analytics traceback that single-blockchain monitoring tools depend on. Several cases involved aggregate value exceeding USD 8.4 million, structured into 14-22 individual bridge transactions sized between USD 180,000 and USD 600,000 to remain below internal compliance thresholds at the originating VASP. Indicative red flags reported by member jurisdictions include: rapid sequential use of two or more cross-chain bridges within a 72-hour window; bridge destination addresses that have transacted with sanctioned mixers within the preceding 30 days; and VASP customer behavior shifting from single-asset trading to multi-chain bridging without a documented commercial rationale.

extracted_typologies.name: Cross-Chain Laundering
extracted_typologies.definition: Cross-chain laundering uses bridge protocols, atomic swaps, and decentralized exchange routing to move illicit virtual-asset proceeds between separate blockchain networks. By hopping chains, the subject defeats chain-analytics traceback that depends on observing a continuous flow on a single ledger. Privacy-enhancing tools (mixers, batching contracts) are frequently layered on top of the bridging activity to further fragment the path. (FATF Mid-Year Review 2025, §4.7.)
extracted_typologies.example: A subject deposits 130 BTC at a Bitcoin-native VASP, bridges the value to USDT on Tron via a cross-chain bridge, then moves the resulting USDT through a Solana mixer before final off-ramping at a different VASP. Aggregate value across the chain involved approximately USD 8.4 million split across 18 bridge transactions sized USD 220,000 to USD 580,000.
